resources.string: No charge sessions yet — plug in and charge for at least a minute.
Why lower than the charger says?

WattFlow measures battery-side power — what actually enters the battery. Chargers display their own output. In between: conversion losses (~10% wired, 30–40% wireless) and the power your screen and system consume while charging. Phones also only draw what their charging protocol negotiates at the current battery level, no matter how powerful the charger is.
Peak In/Out here vs. the widget

These peaks reset every time you unplug, plug back in, or switch source (e.g. wired to wireless) — they cover only the current streak. The home screen widget shows a separate \"Today\'s Peaks\" figure that covers the whole day instead.
Charge history, statistics and background recording. One-time purchase, yours forever.
Battery at %1$d%% — unplug to protect battery life

Lithium batteries age fastest at the extremes. Staying roughly between 20% and 80% reduces voltage stress and heat — the two main causes of permanent capacity loss. An occasional full charge is fine (it helps the gauge calibrate), but sitting at 100% or draining to 0% every day measurably shortens battery lifespan. That\'s why WattFlow defaults to alerts at 80% and 20%.
Live battery power at a glance
No battery sessions yet — they record while the app is open or background recording is on.
When off, sessions record only while the app is open.

Screen-off power saving briefly pauses sampling. By default, WattFlow merges same-direction sessions separated by gaps under 5 minutes — closer to how you think about one charge or one drain. Turn this on to see every raw segment exactly as sampled, split at every gap over 60 seconds.

Exempts WattFlow from battery optimization so background sampling is not paused while the screen is off. Recording becomes more complete, at the cost of slightly higher standby drain (~0.5-1% per hour). On MIUI/HyperOS and similar systems you may also need to allow autostart in system settings.
WattFlow works fine without this permission — screen-off periods may briefly lack samples, shown as separate segments in raw mode.
Only records while you use the app — unlock Pro for complete background recording.

Background recording shows an ongoing notification with the live wattage — Android requires notification permission for it to be visible. Recording works either way; without permission the notification is simply hidden.

Phones with two cells in series often report only half the real power during fast charging, yet true values at low power. WattFlow never adjusts readings automatically. If your fast-charge watts are about half the charger rating, turn on the ×2 correction.

This phone looks like a dual-cell design that may under-report power during fast charging. WattFlow has not changed any readings. Compare with your charger while fast charging; if the number is about half, enable the ×2 correction in Settings.
Does the hint not match your phone? Report your device — it opens a pre-filled page that you review and send yourself.
Draggable live-watts pill over other apps while background recording is on. Needs the display-over-apps permission — the only optional permission WattFlow ever asks for.
Measures this charger + cable combo for 60 seconds and grades it. For comparable results: same battery level range, screen on, phone idle.
Plug in a charger to start.
Measuring… %1$d s left. Keep the phone idle.
Save and compare chargers with Pro
No sessions recorded in the last 14 days.
Battery drain between 23:00 and 07:00. Coverage shows how much of each night was actually recorded — turn on background recording for complete nights.
No night-time discharge recorded yet. Enable background recording and leave the phone unplugged overnight.
Each full charge logs the system fuel gauge reading at 100%. The absolute number varies by device — what matters is how it changes over months. Occasional full charges keep this calibrated.
No full charges logged yet. Charge to 100% now and then — each one adds a calibration point.
